narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction stemmed primarily from your Year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom tier of your school that year, a factor that strongly signaled higher risk of bar exam struggles compared to peers who succeeded. While your high family income background was a positive note, aligning with a smaller but successful group of passers, it wasn't enough to offset that concern. Your LSAT score of 33 also pulled in a favorable direction, though it fell short of the average 37.27 seen among those who passed, offering some reassurance about your potential. In contrast, typical admitted students rank much higher in later-year deciles—around the 6th out of 10 for Year 3 passers—showing steadier academic climbs that our model associates with bar success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for passers, so it played a neutral role overall. As a full-time student with your background, you fit a common profile for many who succeed, but the model weighed your dip in Year 3 grades most heavily against you. This isn't a judgment on your effort or potential—it's just how these patterns from past students shaped the outlook. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the typical admitted range, or strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework or targeted prep. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students like you. Many applicants in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded on appeal or reapplication—your high family income could even open doors to test prep resources that make a difference. Keep pushing; this is one data point, not your whole story.
